first_paragraph: The **Greco-Italian War**,[[6]](#cite_note-10) also called the **Italo-Greek War**,[[7]](#cite_note-11) took place between [Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy") and [Greece](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Greece "Kingdom of Greece") from 28 October 1940 to 23 April 1941. This conflict began the [Balkans campaign](/wiki/Balkans_campaign_(World_War_II) "Balkans campaign (World War II)") of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") between the [Axis powers](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers") and the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II"), and eventually turned into the [Battle of Greece](/wiki/Battle_of_Greece "Battle of Greece") with [British](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") and [German](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") involvement. On 10 June 1940, Italy declared war on France and the United Kingdom. By September 1940, the Italians had [invaded France](/wiki/Italian_invasion_of_France "Italian invasion of France"), [British Somaliland](/wiki/Italian_invasion_of_British_Somaliland "Italian invasion of British Somaliland") and [Egypt](/wiki/Italian_invasion_of_Egypt "Italian invasion of Egypt"). This was followed by a hostile press campaign in Italy against Greece, accused of being a British ally. A number of provocations culminated in the sinking of the Greek light cruiser [*Elli*](/wiki/Greek_cruiser_Elli_(1912) "Greek cruiser Elli (1912)") by the Italians on 15 August. On 28 October, [Mussolini](/wiki/Mussolini "Mussolini") issued an ultimatum to Greece demanding the cession of Greek territory, which the [Prime Minister of Greece](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Greece "Prime Minister of Greece"), [Ioannis Metaxas](/wiki/Ioannis_Metaxas "Ioannis Metaxas"), rejected.

The **Greco-Italian War**,[[6]](#cite_note-10) also called the **Italo-Greek War**,[[7]](#cite_note-11) took place between [Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy") and [Greece](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Greece "Kingdom of Greece") from 28 October 1940 to 23 April 1941. This conflict began the [Balkans campaign](/wiki/Balkans_campaign_(World_War_II) "Balkans campaign (World War II)") of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") between the [Axis powers](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers") and the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II"), and eventually turned into the [Battle of Greece](/wiki/Battle_of_Greece "Battle of Greece") with [British](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") and [German](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") involvement. On 10 June 1940, Italy declared war on France and the United Kingdom. By September 1940, the Italians had [invaded France](/wiki/Italian_invasion_of_France "Italian invasion of France"), [British Somaliland](/wiki/Italian_invasion_of_British_Somaliland "Italian invasion of British Somaliland") and [Egypt](/wiki/Italian_invasion_of_Egypt "Italian invasion of Egypt"). This was followed by a hostile press campaign in Italy against Greece, accused of being a British ally. A number of provocations culminated in the sinking of the Greek light cruiser [*Elli*](/wiki/Greek_cruiser_Elli_(1912) "Greek cruiser Elli (1912)") by the Italians on 15 August. On 28 October, [Mussolini](/wiki/Mussolini "Mussolini") issued an ultimatum to Greece demanding the cession of Greek territory, which the [Prime Minister of Greece](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Greece "Prime Minister of Greece"), [Ioannis Metaxas](/wiki/Ioannis_Metaxas "Ioannis Metaxas"), rejected.

Italy's invasion of Greece, launched with the divisions of the [Royal Army](/wiki/Royal_Italian_Army_during_World_War_II "Royal Italian Army during World War II") based in [Italian-controlled Albania](/wiki/Italian_protectorate_of_Albania_(1939%E2%80%931943) "Italian protectorate of Albania (1939–1943)"), badly armed and poorly commanded, resulted in a setback: the Italian forces encountered unexpectedly tenacious resistance by the [Hellenic Army](/wiki/Hellenic_Army "Hellenic Army") and penetrated only a few kilometers into Greek territory and had to contend with the mountainous and muddy terrain on the Albanian–Greek border. With British air and material support, the Greeks stopped the Italian invasion just inside Greek territory by mid-November and subsequently counter-attacked with the bulk of their mobilized army to push the Italians back into Albania – an advance which culminated in the [Capture of Klisura Pass](/wiki/Capture_of_Klisura_Pass "Capture of Klisura Pass") in January 1941, a few dozen kilometers inside the Albanian border. The defeat of the Italian invasion and the [Greek counter-offensive of 1940](/wiki/Battle_of_Pindus "Battle of Pindus") have been called the "first Axis setback of the entire war" by [Mark Mazower](/wiki/Mark_Mazower "Mark Mazower").

The front stabilized in February 1941, by which time the Italians had reinforced the Albanian front to 28 divisions against the Greeks' 14 divisions (though Greek divisions were larger). In March, the Italians conducted the [unsuccessful spring offensive](/wiki/Italian_spring_offensive "Italian spring offensive"). At this point, losses were mutually costly, but the Greeks had far less ability than the Italians to replenish their losses in both men and material, and they were dangerously low on ammunition and other supplies. They also lacked the ability to rotate out their men and equipment, unlike the Italians.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStockingsHancock2013120–122-12) On the other side the Italian equipment proved to be of poor quality and of little use, while Italian morale remained low throughout the campaign.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHall2014117-13)

[Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") decided that the increased British intervention in the conflict represented a threat to Germany's rear,[[e]](#cite_note-14) while German build-up in the Balkans accelerated after [Bulgaria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Bulgaria "Kingdom of Bulgaria") joined the Axis on 1 March 1941. British ground forces began arriving in Greece the next day. This caused Hitler to come to the aid of his Axis ally. On 6 April, the Germans invaded northern Greece ("[Operation Marita](/wiki/Operation_Marita "Operation Marita")"). The Greeks had deployed the vast majority of their men into a mutually costly stalemate with the Italians on the Albanian front, leaving the fortified [Metaxas Line](/wiki/Metaxas_Line "Metaxas Line") with only a third of its authorized strength.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStockingsHancock201387-15) Greek and British forces in northern Greece were overwhelmed and the Germans advanced rapidly west and south. In Albania, the Greek army made a belated withdrawal to avoid being cut off by the Germans but was followed up by the Italians. Greece surrendered to German troops on 20 April 1941 and to the Italians on 23 April.[[f]](#cite_note-16) Greece was [subsequently occupied](/wiki/Axis_occupation_of_Greece "Axis occupation of Greece") by Bulgarian, German and Italian troops. The Italian army suffered 102,064 combat casualties (with 13,755 dead and 3,900 missing) and fifty thousand wounded; the Greeks suffered over 83,500 combat casualties (including 13,325 killed and 1,200 missing) and 42,000 wounded.[[11]](#cite_note-17)

## Background

### Italian imperialism

Main article: [Italian imperialism under fascism](/wiki/Italian_imperialism_under_fascism "Italian imperialism under fascism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Italy_aims_Europe_1936.png/330px-Italy_aims_Europe_1936.png)](/wiki/File:Italy_aims_Europe_1936.png)

Ambitions of [Fascist Italy](/wiki/Fascist_Italy_(1922%E2%80%931943) "Fascist Italy (1922–1943)") in Europe in 1936.  
Legend:

Metropolitan Italy and dependent territories;

[Client states](/wiki/Client_state "Client state");

Claimed territories to be annexed;

Territories to be transformed into client states.

[Albania](/wiki/Albania "Albania"), which was a client state, was considered a territory to be annexed.

In the late 1920s, Italian Prime Minister [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini") said that [Fascist Italy](/wiki/Fascist_Italy_(1922%E2%80%931943) "Fascist Italy (1922–1943)") needed *[Spazio vitale](/wiki/Spazio_vitale "Spazio vitale")*, an outlet for its surplus population and that it would be in the best interests of other countries to aid in the expansion of [Imperial Italy](/wiki/Italian_imperialism_under_fascism "Italian imperialism under fascism").[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMack_Smith1982170-18) The regime wanted hegemony in the [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean_Basin "Mediterranean Basin")–Danubian–[Balkan](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans") region and Mussolini imagined the conquest "of an empire stretching from the [Strait of Gibraltar](/wiki/Strait_of_Gibraltar "Strait of Gibraltar") to the [Strait of Hormuz](/wiki/Strait_of_Hormuz "Strait of Hormuz")".[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartel1999184,_198-19)

There were designs for a protectorate over the [Albanian Kingdom](/wiki/Albanian_Kingdom_(1928%E2%80%931939) "Albanian Kingdom (1928–1939)") and for the annexation of [Dalmatia](/wiki/Dalmatia "Dalmatia") and economic and military control of the [Kingdom of Yugoslavia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Yugoslavia "Kingdom of Yugoslavia") and the [Kingdom of Greece](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Greece "Kingdom of Greece"). The fascist regime also sought to establish protectorates over the [First Austrian Republic](/wiki/First_Austrian_Republic "First Austrian Republic"), the [Kingdom of Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary_(1920%E2%80%931946) "Kingdom of Hungary (1920–1946)"), the [Kingdom of Romania](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Romania "Kingdom of Romania") and the [Kingdom of Bulgaria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Bulgaria "Kingdom of Bulgaria"), which lay on the periphery of an Italian European [sphere of influence](/wiki/Sphere_of_influence "Sphere of influence").[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBideleuxJeffries1998467-20)

In 1935, Italy began the [Second Italo-Ethiopian War](/wiki/Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War "Second Italo-Ethiopian War") to expand the empire; a more aggressive Italian foreign policy which "exposed [the] vulnerabilities" of the British and French and created an opportunity the Fascist regime needed to realize its imperial goals.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBell199770–71-21)[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartel1999198-22) In 1936, the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War") began and Italy made a military contribution so vast that it played a decisive role in the victory of the [rebel forces](/wiki/Nationalist_faction_(Spanish_Civil_War) "Nationalist faction (Spanish Civil War)") of [Francisco Franco](/wiki/Francisco_Franco "Francisco Franco").[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrestonMacKenzie199621–22-23) "A full-scale external war" was fought for Spanish subservience to the Italian Empire to place Italy on a war footing and to create "a warrior culture".[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPrestonMacKenzie199622,_50–51-24)

In September 1938, the Italian army had made plans to [invade Albania](/wiki/Italian_invasion_of_Albania "Italian invasion of Albania"), which began on 7 April 1939, and in three days had occupied most of the country. Albania was a territory that Italy could acquire for "*living space* to ease its overpopulation" as well as a foothold for expansion in the Balkans.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZabecki19991353-25) [Italy invaded France](/wiki/Italian_invasion_of_France "Italian invasion of France") in June 1940, followed by [their invasion of Egypt](/wiki/Italian_invasion_of_Egypt "Italian invasion of Egypt") in September.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnox2000a181–182-26) A plan to invade Yugoslavia was drawn up, but postponed due to opposition from [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") and a lack of Italian army transport.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnox2000a78–79-27)

### Greek–Italian relations in the interwar period

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/%CE%95%CE%BB%CE%B5%CF%85%CE%B8%CE%AD%CF%81%CE%B9%CE%BF%CF%82_%CE%92%CE%B5%CE%BD%CE%B9%CE%B6%CE%AD%CE%BB%CE%BF%CF%82.jpg/250px-%CE%95%CE%BB%CE%B5%CF%85%CE%B8%CE%AD%CF%81%CE%B9%CE%BF%CF%82_%CE%92%CE%B5%CE%BD%CE%B9%CE%B6%CE%AD%CE%BB%CE%BF%CF%82.jpg)](/wiki/File:%CE%95%CE%BB%CE%B5%CF%85%CE%B8%CE%AD%CF%81%CE%B9%CE%BF%CF%82_%CE%92%CE%B5%CE%BD%CE%B9%CE%B6%CE%AD%CE%BB%CE%BF%CF%82.jpg)

[Eleftherios Venizelos](/wiki/Eleftherios_Venizelos "Eleftherios Venizelos"), Prime Minister of Greece (various terms 1910–1933)

Italy had captured the predominantly [Greek](/wiki/Greeks "Greeks")-inhabited [Dodecanese](/wiki/Dodecanese "Dodecanese") Islands in the [Aegean Sea](/wiki/Aegean_Sea "Aegean Sea") from the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") in the [Italo-Turkish War](/wiki/Italo-Turkish_War "Italo-Turkish War") of 1912. It had occupied them since, after reneging on the 1919 [Venizelos](/wiki/Eleftherios_Venizelos "Eleftherios Venizelos")–[Tittoni](/wiki/Tommaso_Tittoni "Tommaso Tittoni") agreement to cede them to Greece.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVerzijl1970396-28) When the Italians found that Greece had been [promised land in Anatolia](/wiki/Occupation_of_Smyrna "Occupation of Smyrna") at the [Paris Peace Conference, 1919](/wiki/Paris_Peace_Conference,_1919 "Paris Peace Conference, 1919"), for aid in the defeat of the Ottoman Empire during the [First World War](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), the Italian delegation withdrew from the conference for several months. Italy occupied parts of [Anatolia](/wiki/Anatolia "Anatolia") which threatened the Greek occupation zone and the city of [Smyrna](/wiki/Smyrna "Smyrna"). Greek troops were landed and the [Greco-Turkish War (1919–22)](/wiki/Greco-Turkish_War_(1919%E2%80%9322) "Greco-Turkish War (1919–22)") began with Greek troops advanced into Anatolia. Turkish forces eventually defeated the Greeks and with Italian aid, recovered the lost territory, including Smyrna.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPlowman2013910-29) In 1923, Mussolini used the murder of an Italian general on the Greco-Albanian border as a pretext to [bombard and temporarily occupy](/wiki/Corfu_incident "Corfu incident") [Corfu](/wiki/Corfu "Corfu"), the most important of the [Ionian Islands](/wiki/Ionian_Islands "Ionian Islands").[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBell199768-30)

The Greek defeat in Anatolia and the signing of the [Treaty of Lausanne](/wiki/Treaty_of_Lausanne "Treaty of Lausanne") (1923) ended the expansionist *[Megali Idea](/wiki/Megali_Idea "Megali Idea")*. Henceforth Greek foreign policy was largely aimed at preserving the *status quo*. Territorial claims to [Northern Epirus](/wiki/Northern_Epirus "Northern Epirus") (southern Albania), the Italian-ruled Dodecanese, and British-ruled [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus "Cyprus") remained open but inactive in view of the country's weakness and isolation. The main threat Greece faced was from [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria "Bulgaria"), which claimed Greece's northern territories. The years after 1923 were marked by almost complete diplomatic isolation and unresolved disputes with practically every neighbouring country.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESvolopoulos1978342–343-31) The dictatorship of [Theodoros Pangalos](/wiki/Theodoros_Pangalos_(general) "Theodoros Pangalos (general)") in 1925–26 sought to revise the Treaty of Lausanne by a war with Turkey. To this end, Pangalos sought Italian diplomatic support, as Italy still had ambitions in Anatolia, but in the event, nothing came of his overtures to Mussolini.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKlapsis2014240–259-32) After the fall of Pangalos and the restoration of relative political stability in 1926, efforts were undertaken to normalize relations with Turkey, Yugoslavia, Albania and Romania, without much success at first. The same period saw Greece draw closer to Britain and away from France, exacerbated by a dispute over the two sides' financial claims from World War I.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESvolopoulos1978343–345-33)

The Greek government put renewed emphasis on improving [relations with Italy](/wiki/Greece%E2%80%93Italy_relations "Greece–Italy relations") and in November 1926, a trade agreement was signed between the two states. Initiated and energetically pursued by [Andreas Michalakopoulos](/wiki/Andreas_Michalakopoulos "Andreas Michalakopoulos"), the Italian–Greek rapprochement had a positive impact on Greek relations with Romania and Turkey and after 1928 was continued by the new government of [Eleftherios Venizelos](/wiki/Eleftherios_Venizelos "Eleftherios Venizelos").[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESvolopoulos1978345–347-34) This policy culminated with the signing of a treaty of friendship on 23 September 1928.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESvolopoulos1978348-35)[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKitromilides2008217-36) Mussolini exploited this treaty, as it aided in his efforts to diplomatically isolate Yugoslavia from potential Balkan allies. An offer of alliance between the two countries was rebuffed by Venizelos but during the talks Mussolini personally offered "to guarantee Greek sovereignty" on Macedonia and assured Venizelos that in case of an external attack on [Thessaloniki](/wiki/Thessaloniki "Thessaloniki") by Yugoslavia, Italy would join Greece.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKitromilides2008217-36)[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESvolopoulos1978349-37)[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESteiner2005499-38)

During the late 1920s and early 1930s, Mussolini sought diplomatically to create "an Italian-dominated Balkan bloc that would link [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey"), Greece, Bulgaria, and Hungary". Venizelos countered the policy with diplomatic agreements among Greek neighbours and established an "annual Balkan conference ... to study questions of common interest, particularly of an economic nature, with the ultimate aim of establishing some kind of regional union". This increased diplomatic relations and by 1934 was resistant to "all forms of territorial revisionism".[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESteiner2005499–500-39) Venizelos adroitly maintained a principle of "open diplomacy" and was careful not to alienate traditional Greek patrons in Britain and France.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESvolopoulos1978349–350-40) The Greco-Italian friendship agreement ended Greek diplomatic isolation and led to a series of bilateral agreements, most notably the Greco-Turkish Friendship Convention in 1930. This process culminated in the signature of the [Balkan Pact](/wiki/Balkan_Pact "Balkan Pact") between Greece, Yugoslavia, Turkey and Romania, which was a counter to Bulgarian revisionism.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESvolopoulos1978352–358-41)

The [Second Italo-Ethiopian War](/wiki/Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War "Second Italo-Ethiopian War") marked a renewal of Italian expansionism, and began a period where Greece increasingly sought a firm British commitment for its security. Although Britain offered guarantees to Greece (as well as Turkey and Yugoslavia) for the duration of the Ethiopian crisis, it was unwilling to commit itself further so as to avoid limiting its freedom of manoeuvre vis-à-vis Italy.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoliopoulos1978380–381-42) Furthermore, with the (British-backed) restoration of the Greek monarchy in 1935 in the person of the anglophile King [George II](/wiki/George_II_of_Greece "George II of Greece"), Britain had secured its dominant influence in the country. This did not change after the establishment of the dictatorial [4th of August Regime](/wiki/4th_of_August_Regime "4th of August Regime") of [Ioannis Metaxas](/wiki/Ioannis_Metaxas "Ioannis Metaxas") in 1936. Although imitating the Fascist regime in Italy in its ideology and outward appearance, the regime lacked a mass popular base, and its main pillar was the King, who commanded the allegiance of the army.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoliopoulos1978381–391-43) Greek foreign policy thus remained aligned with that of Britain, despite the parallel ever-growing economic penetration of the country by [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany"). Metaxas himself, although an ardent [Germanophile](/wiki/Germanophile "Germanophile") in World War I, followed this line, and after the [Munich Conference](/wiki/Munich_Conference "Munich Conference") in October 1938 suggested a British–Greek alliance to the British ambassador, arguing that Greece "should prepare for the eventuality of a war between Great Britain and Italy, which sooner or later Greece would find itself drawn into". Loath to be embroiled in a possible Greek–Bulgarian war, dismissive of Greece's military ability, and disliking the regime, the British rebuffed the offer.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoliopoulos1978397–402-44)

### Prelude to war, 1939–40

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Mussolini_mezzobusto.jpg/250px-Mussolini_mezzobusto.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mussolini_mezzobusto.jpg)

[Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini"), Prime Minister of Italy

On 4 February 1939, Mussolini addressed the [Fascist Grand Council](/wiki/Grand_Council_of_Fascism "Grand Council of Fascism") on foreign policy. The speech outlined Mussolini's belief that Italy was being imprisoned by France and the United Kingdom and what territory would be needed to break free. During this speech, Mussolini declared Greece to be a "vital [enemy] of Italy and its expansion."[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGooch2007451-45) On 18 March, as signs for an imminent Italian invasion of Albania as well as a possible attack on Corfu mounted, Metaxas wrote in his diary of his determination to resist any Italian attack.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPetraki201418–19-46)

Following the [Italian invasion of Albania](/wiki/Italian_invasion_of_Albania "Italian invasion of Albania") in April, relations between Italy and Greece deteriorated rapidly. The Greeks began making defensive preparations for an Italian attack, while the Italians began improving infrastructure in Albania to facilitate troop movements.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStockingsHancock201321–22-47) The new Italian ambassador, [Emanuele Grazzi](/wiki/Emanuele_Grazzi "Emanuele Grazzi"), arrived in Athens later in April. During his tenure, Grazzi worked earnestly for the improvement of Italian–Greek relations, something that Metaxas too desired—despite his anglophile stance, Grazzi considered him "the only real friend Italy could claim in Greece"—but he was in the awkward position of being ignorant of his country's actual policy towards Greece: he had arrived with no instructions whatsoever, and was constantly left out of the loop thereafter, frequently receiving no replies to his dispatches.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETsirpanlis198230–33,_42-48) Tensions mounted as a result of a continued [anti-Greek](/wiki/Anti-Greek_sentiment "Anti-Greek sentiment") campaign in the Italian press, combined with provocative Italian actions. Thus during Foreign Minister [Galeazzo Ciano](/wiki/Galeazzo_Ciano "Galeazzo Ciano")'s visit to Albania, posters supporting Albanian [irredentism](/wiki/Irredentism "Irredentism") in [Chameria](/wiki/Chameria "Chameria") were publicly displayed; the governor of the Italian Dodecanese, [Cesare Maria De Vecchi](/wiki/Cesare_Maria_De_Vecchi "Cesare Maria De Vecchi"), closed the remaining Greek communal schools in the province, and Italian troops were heard singing "*Andremo nell'Egeo, prenderemo pure il Pireo. E, se tutto va bene, prenderemo anche Aténe.*" ("We go to the Aegean, and will take even Piraeus. And if all goes well, we will take Athens too."). Four of the five Italian divisions in Albania moved towards the Greek border, and on 16 August the Italian Chief of the General Staff, Marshal [Pietro Badoglio](/wiki/Pietro_Badoglio "Pietro Badoglio"), received orders to begin planning for an attack on Greece. On 4 August, Metaxas had ordered Greek forces to a state of readiness and a partial mobilization.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECervi19727–9-49)[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPetraki201421–22,_24,_293_(note_16)-50)

> The entire road-building programme has been directed towards the Greek border. And this is by order of the Duce, who is thinking more and more of attacking Greece at the first opportunity.

Entry in Ciano's diary for 12 May 1939[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETsirpanlis198242-51)

Although both Britain and France publicly guaranteed the independence of Greece and Romania on 13 April 1939, the British still refused to be drawn into concrete undertakings towards Greece, as they hoped to entice Mussolini to remain neutral in the coming conflict with Germany, and saw in a potential Greek alliance only a drain on their own resources.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoliopoulos1978402–403,_406-52) With British encouragement, Metaxas made diplomatic overtures to Italy in August, and on 12 September, Mussolini wrote to Metaxas, assuring him that if he entered the war, Italy would respect Greek neutrality, and that Italian troops based in Albania would be pulled back about 30 kilometres (20 miles) from the Greek border. The Italian dictator even instructed Grazzi, to express his trust towards Metaxas and offer to sell Greece aircraft.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECervi19727–10-53)[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoliopoulos1978403–404-54) On 20 September, the Italians offered to formalize relations by renewing the 1928 treaty. Metaxas rejected this, as the British Foreign Office was opposed to a formal commitment by Greece to Italy, and made only a public declaration of friendship and good-will. Greek–Italian relations entered a friendly phase that lasted until spring 1940.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoliopoulos1978404–405-55)[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPetraki201428–29-56)

In May 1940, as Italian entry into the war became imminent, the Italian press began an [anti-Greek](/wiki/Anti-Greek_sentiment "Anti-Greek sentiment") propaganda campaign, accusing the country of being a foreign puppet and tolerating British warships in its waters.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPetraki201430-57) Following the [defeat of France](/wiki/Battle_of_France "Battle of France"), Greek–Italian relations deteriorated further. From 18 June, De Vecchi sent a series of protests to Rome, reporting on the presence of British warships in Crete and other Greek islands and claimed that a British base had been established at [Milos](/wiki/Milos "Milos").[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnox1986139-58) The allegations were overblown but not entirely unjustified: in January 1940, bowing to British pressure, Greece concluded a trade agreement with Britain, limiting its exports to Germany and allowing Britain to use the large Greek merchant fleet for its war effort, marking Greece a tacit member of the anti-Axis camp, despite its official neutrality.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoliopoulos1978406–408-59) British warships did sail deep into the Aegean, leading the British ambassador in Athens to recommend, on 17 August, that the government put a stop to them.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPetraki2014299_(note_69)-60) Mussolini saw his war as a *guerra parallela* ("parallel war") under which Italy would finally conquer its *spazio vitale* allied to Germany, but without the help of Germany since until early 1941 he remained vehemently opposed to the Wehrmacht operating in the Mediterranean.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKallis2000175-61) As such, he wanted Italy to occupy all the territory that he saw as part of Italy's *spazio vitale*, including in the Balkans, before Germany won the expected victory over Britain.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKallis2000176-62) The consistent German opposition to any Italian move into the Balkans was a major irritant to Mussolini as he saw it as a German attempt to block Italy from getting its fair share of the spoils before the war was won.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKallis2000176-62) In July 1940, Mussolini was forced under German pressure to cancel a planned invasion of Yugoslavia (an important source of raw materials for the *Reich*), which was frustrating to him as he long had designs on Yugoslav territory.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKallis2000176-62)

Italian military forces harassed Greek forces with air attacks on Greek naval vessels at sea.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETucker2012323-63) On 12 July, while attacking a British petrol carrier off Crete, Italian aircraft based in the Dodecanese went on to bombard Greek warships in harbour at [Kissamos](/wiki/Kissamos "Kissamos"). On 31 July Italian bombers attacked two Greek destroyers in the [Gulf of Corinth](/wiki/Gulf_of_Corinth "Gulf of Corinth") and two submarines in [Nafpaktos](/wiki/Nafpaktos "Nafpaktos"); two days later a coastguard vessel was attacked at [Aegina](/wiki/Aegina "Aegina"), off Athens.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPetraki201433–34-64)[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnox1986167–168-65) Ciano's diary confirms that over the summer of 1940, Mussolini turned his attention to the Balkans: on 6 August, Mussolini was planning an attack on Yugoslavia, while on 10–12 August he railed against the Greeks, promising to rectify the "unfinished business" of 1923.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETsirpanlis198242-51) Count Ciano was the Italian official who had pushed most strongly for the conquest of Albania in 1939 and afterwards Albania was ruled very much as his own "personal fiefdom" as the viceroy [Francesco Jacomoni](/wiki/Francesco_Jacomoni "Francesco Jacomoni") was a lackey of Ciano's.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw2007164-66) As a way of improving his prestige within the regime, Ciano was the Italian official who pressed the hardest for the invasion of Greece as he saw conquering Greece (an invasion that would have to be launched from Albania) as a way of showing off just how well run Albania was under his rule.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw2007164-66) On 10 August 1940, Ciano met Mussolini to tell him the story of the Albanian bandit Daut Hoxha, whom Ciano presented to Mussolini as a pro-Italian Albanian patriot murdered by the Greeks.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw2007164-66) In reality, Hoxha was a cattle-thief with a "long history of extreme violence and criminality" who had been beheaded by a rival gang of Albanian bandits.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw2007164-66) As intended, Ciano's story worked Mussolini into a state of rage against the Greeks, with Ciano writing in his diary: "The Duce is considering an 'act of force because since 1923 [the Corfu incident] he has some accounts to settle and the Greeks deceive themselves if they think he has forgotten'".[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw2007164-66)

On 11 August, orchestrated by Ciano and the Italian viceroy in Albania, [Francesco Jacomoni](/wiki/Francesco_Jacomoni "Francesco Jacomoni"), the Italian and Albanian press began a campaign against Greece, on the pretext of the murder of the bandit Daut Hoxha in June.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw2007164-66) Hoxha was presented as a patriot fighting for the liberty of Chameria and his murder the work of Greek agents. Ciano wrote approvingly in his diary that Mussolini wanted more information on Ciamuria (the Italian term for Epirus) and had ordered both Jacomoni and General Count [Sebastiano Visconti Prasca](/wiki/Sebastiano_Visconti_Prasca "Sebastiano Visconti Prasca") Guzzoni to Rome.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw2007165-67) Visconti Prasca, the aristocratic commander of the *Regio Esercito* forces in Albania was a bodybuilder excessively proud of his "manly physique" who neglected his military duties in favor of physical exercises, and promptly told Mussolini that his forces were more than capable of conquering Greece.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw2007165-67) Although Greek "expansionism" was denounced and claims for the surrender of Chameria made, Ciano and well-informed German sources regarded the press campaign as a means to intimidate Greece, rather than a prelude to war.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPetraki201434–35-68)[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnox1986170–173-69)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Cruiser_Elli.jpg/250px-Cruiser_Elli.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cruiser_Elli.jpg)

[Greek cruiser *Elli*](/wiki/Greek_cruiser_Elli_(1912) "Greek cruiser Elli (1912)") that was sunk on 15 August 1940 while she sat at anchor.

On 15 August 1940 (the [Dormition of the Theotokos](/wiki/Dormition_of_the_Theotokos "Dormition of the Theotokos"), a Greek national religious holiday), the Greek light cruiser *Elli* was sunk by the Italian submarine *[Delfino](/wiki/Italian_submarine_Delfino_(1930) "Italian submarine Delfino (1930)")* in [Tinos](/wiki/Tinos "Tinos") harbour. The sinking was a result of orders by Mussolini and Navy chief [Domenico Cavagnari](/wiki/Domenico_Cavagnari "Domenico Cavagnari") allowing submarine attacks on neutral shipping. This was taken up by De Vecchi, who ordered the *Delfino*'s commander to "sink everything in sight in the vicinity of Tinos and [Syros](/wiki/Syros "Syros")", giving the impression that war was imminent. On the same day, another Greek steamship was bombarded by Italian planes in Crete.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPetraki201435–37-70)[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnox1986173–174-71) Despite evidence of Italian responsibility, the Greek government announced that the attack had been carried out by a submarine of unknown nationality. No-one was fooled and the sinking of *Elli* outraged the Greek people. Ambassador Grazzi wrote in his memoirs that the attack united a people "deeply riven by unbridgeable political differences and old and deep-running political hatreds" and imbued them with a firm resolve to resist.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPetraki201437–38-72) Grazzi's position was particularly problematic: a firm believer in Italian–Greek friendship, and unaware of Ciano's shift towards war, he tried his best to smooth over problems and avoid a conflict. As a result, Metaxas, who believed Grazzi to be a "faithful executor of Rome's orders", was left unsure of Italy's true intentions, wavering between optimism and "crises of prudent rationalism", in the words of Tsirpanlis. Neither Metaxas nor Grazzi realized that the latter was being kept in his post "deliberately in order to allay the suspicions of the Greek government and so that the aggressive plans against Greece might remain concealed".[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETsirpanlis198233–35-73)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Ioannis_Metaxas_1937_cropped.jpg/250px-Ioannis_Metaxas_1937_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ioannis_Metaxas_1937_cropped.jpg)

[Ioannis Metaxas](/wiki/Ioannis_Metaxas "Ioannis Metaxas"), Prime Minister of Greece (1936–1941)

German intervention, urging Italy to avoid Balkan complications and concentrate on Britain, along with the start of the [Italian invasion of Egypt](/wiki/Italian_invasion_of_Egypt "Italian invasion of Egypt"), led to the postponement of Italian ambitions in Greece and Yugoslavia: on 22 August, Mussolini postponed the attack on Greece for the end of September, and for 20 October on Yugoslavia.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETsirpanlis198242-51)[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnox1986174–177-74) On 7 October, German troops entered Romania, to guard the [Ploiești](/wiki/Ploie%C8%99ti "Ploiești") oil fields and prepare for [Operation Barbarossa](/wiki/Operation_Barbarossa "Operation Barbarossa"). Mussolini, who had not been informed in advance, regarded it as an encroachment on Italy's sphere of influence in the Balkans, and advanced plans for an invasion of Greece.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESadkovich1993439–445-75)[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStockingsHancock201337-76) The fact that Hitler never told Mussolini of any foreign policy moves in advance had long been considered humiliating by the latter and he was determined to strike Greece without informing Hitler as a way of asserting Italian equality with Germany.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKallis2000178-77) On 13 October, Mussolini told Marshal Badoglio that Italy was going to war with Greece, with Badoglio making no objections.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw2007170-78) The next day, Badoglio first learned that Mussolini planned to occupy all of Greece instead of just Epirus as he had been led to understand, which led Badoglio to say that the *Regio Esercito* would require 20 divisions in Albania, which in turn would require 3 months, but he did not press this point.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw2007170–171-79) The one man in Italy who could have stopped the war, King [Victor Emmanuel III](/wiki/Victor_Emmanuel_III_of_Italy "Victor Emmanuel III of Italy"), chose to bless it instead.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw2007175-80) The king told Mussolini at a meeting that he had his support as he expected the Greeks to "crumble".[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw2007175-80) Victor Emmanuel was looking forward to having a fourth crown to wear (Mussolini had already given Victor Emmanuel the titles Emperor of Ethiopia and King of the Albanians).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Opposing plans

### Italy

The Italian war aim was to establish a Greek [puppet state](/wiki/Puppet_state "Puppet state"), which would permit the Italian annexation of the Ionian Islands and the [Sporades](/wiki/Sporades "Sporades") and the [Cyclades](/wiki/Cyclades "Cyclades") islands in the [Aegean Sea](/wiki/Aegean_Sea "Aegean Sea"), to be administered as a part of the [Italian Islands of the Aegean](/wiki/Italian_Islands_of_the_Aegean "Italian Islands of the Aegean").[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERodogno2006103–104-81) The islands were claimed on the basis that they had once belonged to the [Venetian Republic](/wiki/Venetian_Republic "Venetian Republic") and the Venetian client state of [Naxos](/wiki/Duchy_of_Naxos "Duchy of Naxos").[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERodogno200684–85-82) The [Epirus](/wiki/Epirus_(region) "Epirus (region)") and [Acarnania](/wiki/Acarnania "Acarnania") regions were to be separated from the rest of the Greek territory and the Italian-controlled [Kingdom of Albania](/wiki/Albanian_Kingdom_(1939%E2%80%9343) "Albanian Kingdom (1939–43)") was to annex territory between the Greek north-western frontier and a line from [Florina](/wiki/Florina "Florina") to [Pindus](/wiki/Pindus "Pindus"), [Arta](/wiki/Arta,_Greece "Arta, Greece") and [Preveza](/wiki/Preveza "Preveza").[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERodogno2006104-83) The Italians intended to partly compensate Greece for its extensive territorial losses by allowing it to annex the [British Crown Colony](/wiki/British_Crown_Colony "British Crown Colony") of [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus "Cyprus") after the war.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnox1986138-84)

On 13 October, Mussolini finalized the decision for war when he informed Marshal Badoglio to start preparing an attack for 26 October. Badoglio then issued the order for the Italian military to begin preparations for executing the existing war plan, "Contingency G[reece]", which envisioned the capture of Epirus as far as Arta but left the further pursuit of the campaign open.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnox1986209-85) On the next day, Badoglio and acting Army Chief of Staff [Mario Roatta](/wiki/Mario_Roatta "Mario Roatta") met with Mussolini, who announced that his objective was the capture of the entire country and that he would contact Bulgaria for a joint operation. Roatta advised that an extension of the invasion beyond Epirus would require an additional ten divisions, which would take three months to arrive and suggested limiting the extent of the Italian demobilization. Both generals urged Mussolini to replace the local commander, Lieutenant-General [Sebastiano Visconti Prasca](/wiki/Sebastiano_Visconti_Prasca "Sebastiano Visconti Prasca"), with someone of greater seniority and experience. Mussolini seemingly agreed but also insisted on the attack going ahead at the determined date, provisionally under Prasca's command.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw2007170-78) Badoglio and Roatta seemed unconvinced that the operation would take place, as with similar projects against Greece and Yugoslavia.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnox1986209–211-86)

The following day Mussolini called another conference, with Badoglio, Roatta, Visconti Prasca, Ciano, and Jacomoni.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw2007170-78) Neither Admiral [Domenico Cavagnari](/wiki/Domenico_Cavagnari "Domenico Cavagnari") of the *Regia Marina* nor [Francesco Pricolo](/wiki/Francesco_Pricolo "Francesco Pricolo") of the *Regia Aeronautica* were asked to attend while Roatta arrived late as he was invited by Mussolini's secretary to the meeting just before it started.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw2007170-78) Mussolini reiterated his objectives; stated he believed that neither of Greece's allies in the Balkan Pact, Yugoslavia or Turkey would act; expressed his determination that the attack take place on 26 October and asked for the opinion of the assembled.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw2007170-78) Jacomoni agreed that the Albanians were enthusiastic but that the Greeks would fight, likely with British help, while Ciano suggested that the Greek people were apathetic and would not support the "plutocratic" ruling class.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnox1986211–212-87) Prasca offered assurances that the operation was as perfectly planned as "humanly possible", and promised to finish off the Greek forces in Epirus (which he estimated at 30,000 men) and capture the port of Preveza in ten to fifteen days.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnox1986212-88)[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGedeon20018-89) Prasca regarded the campaign as an opportunity to win fame and achieve the coveted rank of [Marshal of Italy](/wiki/Marshal_of_Italy "Marshal of Italy") by conquering Athens. He was relatively junior in his rank and knew that if he demanded more troops for the Albanian front, it was likely that a more senior officer would be sent to command the operation, earning the accolades and promotions instead.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGedeon20018,_10–11-90)

During the discussion only Badoglio voiced objections, pointing out that stopping after seizing Epirus—which he conceded would present little difficulty—would be an error, and that a force of at least twenty divisions would be necessary to conquer the whole country, including [Crete](/wiki/Crete "Crete"), through he did not criticize Prasca's plans.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw2007171–172-91) Badoglio also stated he believed it was very unlikely that Britain would send forces to Greece and wanted an Italian offensive into Egypt to be timed with the invasion of Greece.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw2007172-92) Roatta suggested that the schedule of moving troops to Albania would have to be accelerated and called for two divisions to be sent against Thessaloniki as a diversion. Prasca pointed out the inadequacy of Albanian harbours for the rapid transfer of Italian divisions, the mountainous terrain, and the poor state of the Greek transport network, but remained confident that Athens could be captured after the fall of Epirus, with "five or six divisions".[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnox1986213–214-93) The meeting ended with an outline plan, summed up by Mussolini as "offensive in Epirus; observation and pressure on Salonika, and, in a second phase, march on Athens".[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnox1986214-94) The British historian Ian Kershaw called the meeting at the Palazzo Venezia on 15 October 1940 "one of the most superficial and dilettantish discussions of high-risk military strategy ever recorded".[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw2007171-95) The Greek historian Aristotle Kallis writes that Mussolini in October 1940 "was overpowered by hubris", a supremely overconfident man whose vainglorious pursuit of power led him to believe that under his leadership Italy was about to win as he put it "the glory she has sought in vain for three centuries".[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKallis2000178-77)

The staging of incidents at the border to provide a suitable pretext (analogous to the [Gleiwitz incident](/wiki/Gleiwitz_incident "Gleiwitz incident")) was agreed for 24 October. Mussolini suggested that the expected advance of the 10th Army (Marshal [Rodolfo Graziani](/wiki/Rodolfo_Graziani "Rodolfo Graziani")) on [Mersa Matruh](/wiki/Mersa_Matruh "Mersa Matruh"), in Egypt, be brought forward to prevent the British from aiding Greece.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnox1986212-88) Over the next couple of days Badoglio failed to elicit objections to the attack from the other service chiefs or to achieve its cancellation on technical grounds. Mussolini, enraged by the Marshal's obstructionism, threatened to accept his resignation if offered. Badoglio backed down, managing only to secure a postponement of the attack until 28 October.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnox1986214–216-96)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Pindus_map.JPG/250px-Pindus_map.JPG)](/wiki/File:Pindus_map.JPG)

Pindus mountains outlined

The front was roughly 150 kilometres (90 mi) wide in mountain terrain with very few roads. The Pindus mountains divided it into two theatres of operations, Epirus and [western Macedonia](/wiki/Western_Macedonia "Western Macedonia").[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauer200099-97)[*[verification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] The Italian forces in Albania were organised accordingly: the [XXV *Ciamuria* Corps](/wiki/XXV_Army_Corps_(Italy) "XXV Army Corps (Italy)") (Lieutenant-General [Carlo Rossi](/wiki/Carlo_Rossi_(general) "Carlo Rossi (general)")) in the west was charged with the conquest of Epirus, while the [XXVI *Corizza* Corps](/wiki/XXVI_Army_Corps_(Italy) "XXVI Army Corps (Italy)") (Lieutenant-General [Gabriele Nasci](/wiki/Gabriele_Nasci "Gabriele Nasci")) in the east, around [Korçë](/wiki/Kor%C3%A7%C3%AB "Korçë"), would initially remain passive in the direction of western Macedonia.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGedeon20018–10-98)

On 18 October Mussolini sent a letter to Tsar [Boris III of Bulgaria](/wiki/Boris_III_of_Bulgaria "Boris III of Bulgaria") inviting him to take part in the coming action against Greece, but Boris refused, citing his country's unreadiness and its encirclement by hostile neighbours.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnox1986218-99) This was not regarded as a major setback, as the Italian leadership considered that the threat of Bulgarian intervention alone would compel the Greek High Command to commit most of its army in eastern Macedonia and Thrace. It was not until 24 October that Badoglio realized that not only were the Greeks already mobilizing, but that they were prepared to divert most of their forces to Epirus, leaving only six divisions against Bulgaria.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnox1986218-99) Prasca would still have numerical superiority at the start of the campaign (some 150,000 men against 120,000) but concerns grew over the vulnerability of the left flank. The [29th Infantry Division "Piemonte"](/wiki/29th_Infantry_Division_%22Piemonte%22 "29th Infantry Division \"Piemonte\"") was diverted from the attack in Epirus to bolster XXVI Corps in the Korçë area, while the [19th Infantry Division "Venezia"](/wiki/19th_Infantry_Division_%22Venezia%22 "19th Infantry Division \"Venezia\"") was ordered south from its position along the Yugoslav border.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnox1986218–219-100)

In 1936 General [Alberto Pariani](/wiki/Alberto_Pariani "Alberto Pariani") had been appointed Chief of Staff of the army, and had begun a reorganisation of divisions to fight *wars of rapid decision*, according to thinking that speed, mobility and new technology could revolutionise military operations. In 1937, three-regiment (triangular) divisions began to change to two-regiment ([binary divisions](/wiki/Regio_Esercito_(World_War_II)#Binary_infantry_division "Regio Esercito (World War II)")), as part of a ten years plan to reorganise the standing army into 24 binary, 24 triangular, twelve mountain, three motorised and three armoured divisions.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMaiolo2010197-101) The effect of the change was to increase the administrative overhead of the army, with no corresponding increase in effectiveness, as the new technology of tanks, motor vehicles, and wireless communications was slow to arrive and was inferior to that of potential enemies. The dilution of the officer class by the need for extra unit staffs was made worse by the politicisation of the army and the addition of Blackshirt Militia.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacksey197124-102) The reforms also promoted [frontal assaults](/wiki/Frontal_assault "Frontal assault") to the exclusion of other theories, dropping the previous emphasis on fast mobile warfare backed by artillery.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJowett20004–5-103)

Prior to the invasion Mussolini let 300,000 troops and 600,000 reservists go home for the harvest.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECervi19727–10-53) There were supposed to be 1,750 [lorries](/wiki/Truck "Truck") used in the invasion but only 107 arrived. The possibility that Greek officials situated in the front area could be corrupted or would not react to an invasion proved to be mostly wishful thinking, used by Italian generals and personalities in favor of a military intervention; the same was true for an alleged revolt of the Albanian minority living in [Chameria](/wiki/Chameria "Chameria"), located in the Greek territory immediately behind the boundary, which would break out after the beginning of the attack.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECervi19727–10-53)

On the eve of 28 October 1940, Italy's ambassador in [Athens](/wiki/Athens "Athens"), Emanuele Grazzi, handed an ultimatum from Mussolini to Metaxas. It demanded free passage for his troops to occupy unspecified strategic points inside Greek territory. Greece had been friendly towards Nazi Germany, profiting from mutual trade relations, but now Germany's ally, Italy, intended to invade Greece. Metaxas rejected the ultimatum with the words "*Alors, c'est la guerre*" (French for "then it is war"). In this, he echoed the will of the Greek people to resist, a will that was popularly expressed in one word: "[*ochi*](/wiki/Ohi_Day "Ohi Day")" (Όχι) ([Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") for "no"). Within hours, Italy attacked Greece from Albania. The outbreak of hostilities was first announced by Athens Radio early in the morning of 28 October, with the two-sentence dispatch of the general staff.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

> Since 05:30 this morning, the enemy is attacking our vanguard on the Greek–Albanian border. Our forces are defending the fatherland.

— Greek General Staff, 28 October 1940

### Greece

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/c/ca/Papagos_alexandros.jpg)](/wiki/File:Papagos_alexandros.jpg)

[Alexandros Papagos](/wiki/Alexandros_Papagos "Alexandros Papagos"), commander of the Greek Army

In 1936, the [4th of August Regime](/wiki/4th_of_August_Regime "4th of August Regime") came to power in Greece, under the leadership of [Ioannis Metaxas](/wiki/Ioannis_Metaxas "Ioannis Metaxas"). Plans were laid down for the reorganization of the Greek armed forces, including building the "[Metaxas Line](/wiki/Metaxas_Line "Metaxas Line")'", a defensive fortification along the Greco-Bulgarian frontier. Large sums of money were spent to re-equip the army but due to the increasing threat of and the eventual outbreak of war, the most significant foreign purchases from 1938 to 1939, were only partly delivered or not at all. A massive [contingency plan](/wiki/Contingency_plan "Contingency plan") was developed and great amounts of food and equipment were stockpiled in many parts of the country as a precaution in the event of war. After the Italian occupation of Albania in spring 1939, the Greek General Staff prepared the "IB" (Italy-Bulgaria) plan, anticipating a combined offensive by Italy and Bulgaria. Given the overwhelming superiority of such an alliance in manpower and matériel, the plan prescribed a purely defensive strategy, including the gradual retreat of the Greek forces in Epirus to the [Arachthos River](/wiki/Arachthos_River "Arachthos River")–[Metsovo](/wiki/Metsovo "Metsovo")–Aliakmon River–[Mt. Vermion](/wiki/Vermion_Mountains "Vermion Mountains") line, to gain time for the completion of mobilization.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGedeon20016-104)

With the completion of partial mobilization of the frontier formations, the plan was revised with variants "IBa" (1 September 1939) and "IBb" (20 April 1940). These modified the role of the main Greek force in the region, the [8th Infantry Division](/wiki/8th_Infantry_Division_(Greece) "8th Infantry Division (Greece)") (Major-General [Charalambos Katsimitros](/wiki/Charalambos_Katsimitros "Charalambos Katsimitros")). Plan "IB" foresaw it covering the left flank of the bulk of the Greek forces in western Macedonia, securing the Metsovon pass and blocking entry into [Aetolia-Acarnania](/wiki/Aetolia-Acarnania "Aetolia-Acarnania"), "IBa" ordered the covering of [Ioannina](/wiki/Ioannina "Ioannina") and the defence of the [Kalamas](/wiki/Kalamas "Kalamas") river line. Katsimitros had discretion to choose the defensive line and chose the Kalpaki line, which lay astride the main invasion axis from Albania and allowed him to use the Kalamas swamps to neutralize the Italian tank threat.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGedeon20017-105) The Greek General Staff remained focused on Bulgaria as its main potential enemy: of the 851 million [drachmas](/wiki/Modern_drachma "Modern drachma") spent on fortification between April 1939 and October 1940, only 82 million went to the Albanian frontier and the rest on the Metaxas Line and other works in the north-east.[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoliopoulos1978412-106)

Nevertheless, given the enormous numerical and material superiority of the Italian military, the Greek leadership, from Metaxas down, was reserved and cautious, with few hopes of outright victory in a conflict with Italy. The General Staff's plan for the defence of Epirus envisaged withdrawal to a more defensible line, and it was only through Katsimitros' insistence that the Italian attack was confronted close to the border. Metaxas himself, during a briefing of the press on 30 October 1940, reiterated his unshakeable confidence on the ultimate victory of Britain, and hence of Greece, but was less confident on the short-term prospects, noting that "Greece is not fighting for victory. It is fighting for glory. And for its honour. ... A nation must be able to fight, if it wants to remain great, even with no hope of victory. Just because it has to."[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETsirpanlis1992119–120-107) On the other hand, this pessimism was not shared by the population at large, whose enthusiasm, optimism, and the almost religious indignation at the torpedoing of *Elli*, created an élan that helped transform the conflict in Greece's favour.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETsirpanlis1992112–113,_121–122-108) As late as March 1941, when the German intervention was looming, an Italian officer summed up the Greeks' attitude for Mussolini with the words of a captured Greek officer: "we are sure that we will lose the war, but we will give you the spanking you need".[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETsirpanlis1992128-109)

## Forces

### Italy

In the Epirus sector, the [Army Corps of the Ciamuria](/wiki/XXV_Army_Corps_(Italy) "XXV Army Corps (Italy)"), which operated in the [Chameria](/wiki/Chameria "Chameria") region ([Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *Ciamuria*), and consisted of the [23rd Infantry Division "Ferrara"](/wiki/23rd_Infantry_Division_%22Ferrara%22 "23rd Infantry Division \"Ferrara\"") (12,785 men, 60 guns and 3,500 Albanian auxiliary troops), the [51st Infantry Division "Siena"](/wiki/51st_Infantry_Division_%22Siena%22 "51st Infantry Division \"Siena\"") (9,200 men and 50 guns), the [131st Armored Division "Centauro"](/wiki/131st_Armored_Division_%22Centauro%22 "131st Armored Division \"Centauro\"") (4,037 men, 24 guns and 163 light tanks, of which only 90 operational)., and the [Regiment "Cavalleggeri Guide"](/wiki/Regiment_%22Cavalleggeri_Guide%22 "Regiment \"Cavalleggeri Guide\""). In addition, the army corps was reinforced by the brigade-level Littoral Grouping, which consisted of the [3rd Regiment "Granatieri di Sardegna e d'Albania"](/wiki/3rd_Regiment_%22Granatieri_di_Sardegna_e_d%27Albania%22 "3rd Regiment \"Granatieri di Sardegna e d'Albania\""), [Regiment "Lancieri di Aosta"](/wiki/Regiment_%22Lancieri_di_Aosta%22 "Regiment \"Lancieri di Aosta\"") and [Regiment "Lancieri di Milano"](/wiki/Regiment_%22Lancieri_di_Milano%22 "Regiment \"Lancieri di Milano\""), and operated on the Italian right along the coast (4,823 men and 32 guns). The Army Corps of the [Ciamuria](/wiki/Chameria "Chameria"), which was renamed XXV Army Corps on 17 November 1940, comprised 22 infantry battalions, three cavalry regiments, 61 artillery batteries (18 heavy) and 90 tanks. Along with [Blackshirt](/wiki/Blackshirt "Blackshirt") battalions and auxiliary troops, it numbered c. 42,000 men.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGedeon20019-110) [XXVI Army Corps](/wiki/XXVI_Army_Corps_(Italy) "XXVI Army Corps (Italy)") in the Korçë area comprised the [29th Infantry Division "Piemonte"](/wiki/29th_Infantry_Division_%22Piemonte%22 "29th Infantry Division \"Piemonte\"") (9,300 men and 32 guns), and the [49th Infantry Division "Parma"](/wiki/49th_Infantry_Division_%22Parma%22 "49th Infantry Division \"Parma\"") (12,000 men and 60 guns). In addition, the Corps comprised the [19th Infantry Division "Venezia"](/wiki/19th_Infantry_Division_%22Venezia%22 "19th Infantry Division \"Venezia\"") (10,000 men and 40 guns), moving south from its deployment along the Yugoslav frontier between [Lake Prespa](/wiki/Lake_Prespa "Lake Prespa") and [Elbasan](/wiki/Elbasan "Elbasan"), and was later reinforced with the [53rd Infantry Division "Arezzo"](/wiki/53rd_Infantry_Division_%22Arezzo%22 "53rd Infantry Division \"Arezzo\"") (12,000 men and 32 guns) around [Shkodër](/wiki/Shkod%C3%ABr "Shkodër"). The XXVI Army Corps totalled 32 infantry battalions, about ten tanks and two cavalry companies, 68 batteries (7 heavy) for a total of c. 44,000 men.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGedeon20019–10-111) The [3rd Alpine Division "Julia"](/wiki/3rd_Alpine_Division_%22Julia%22 "3rd Alpine Division \"Julia\"") with (10,800 men and 29 guns), was placed between the corps to cover the advance of XXV Army Corps along the Pindus mountains.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGedeon200110-112) The *[Regia Aeronautica](/wiki/Regia_Aeronautica "Regia Aeronautica")* had 380 aircraft available for operations against Greece.[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArgyle198049-113)
About half of the fighter force consisted of 64 [Fiat CR.42](/wiki/Fiat_CR.42 "Fiat CR.42") *Falco* (Hawk) and 23 [Fiat CR.32](/wiki/Fiat_CR.32 "Fiat CR.32") *Freccia* (Arrow) biplanes (the latter already outdated). More modern and effective were the fifty [Fiat G.50bis](/wiki/Fiat_G.50_Freccia "Fiat G.50 Freccia"), Italian first all-metal fighters, available at the opening of the hostilities. Sixty [CANT Z.1007s](/wiki/CANT_Z.1007 "CANT Z.1007") *Alcione* (Halcyon) represented the bulk of the Italian bomber force. Of wooden construction, these three-engined aircraft could endure a lot of punishment and were highly manoeuvrable. Other trimotors were also based on Albanian airfields: 72 [Savoia-Marchetti SM.81](/wiki/Savoia-Marchetti_SM.81 "Savoia-Marchetti SM.81") *Pipistrello* (Bat), a veteran of [Spanish War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War"), with fixed undercarriage,[[108]](#cite_note-Carr_pp._27-28-114) and 31 [Savoia-Marchetti SM.79](/wiki/Savoia-Marchetti_SM.79 "Savoia-Marchetti SM.79") *Sparviero* (Sparrowhawk) built with steel tubes, timber, aluminium, and fabric and carrying scarce defensive firepower.[[109]](#cite_note-Carr_p._28-115)

### Greece

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/%CE%A3%CF%84%CF%81%CE%B1%CF%84%CE%B9%CF%8E%CF%84%CE%B7%CF%82_%CE%BC%CE%B5_%CF%84%CE%B7_%CE%BC%CE%B7%CF%84%CE%AD%CF%81%CE%B1_%CF%84%CE%BF%CF%85_%CF%80%CF%81%CE%B9%CE%BD_%CF%84%CE%B7%CE%BD_%CE%B1%CE%BD%CE%B1%CF%87%CF%8E%CF%81%CE%B7%CF%83%CE%B7_%CE%B3%CE%B9%CE%B1_%CF%84%CE%BF_%CE%B1%CE%BB%CE%B2%CE%B1%CE%BD%CE%B9%CE%BA%CF%8C_%CE%BC%CE%AD%CF%84%CF%89%CF%80%CE%BF.jpg/250px-%CE%A3%CF%84%CF%81%CE%B1%CF%84%CE%B9%CF%8E%CF%84%CE%B7%CF%82_%CE%BC%CE%B5_%CF%84%CE%B7_%CE%BC%CE%B7%CF%84%CE%AD%CF%81%CE%B1_%CF%84%CE%BF%CF%85_%CF%80%CF%81%CE%B9%CE%BD_%CF%84%CE%B7%CE%BD_%CE%B1%CE%BD%CE%B1%CF%87%CF%8E%CF%81%CE%B7%CF%83%CE%B7_%CE%B3%CE%B9%CE%B1_%CF%84%CE%BF_%CE%B1%CE%BB%CE%B2%CE%B1%CE%BD%CE%B9%CE%BA%CF%8C_%CE%BC%CE%AD%CF%84%CF%89%CF%80%CE%BF.jpg)](/wiki/File:%CE%A3%CF%84%CF%81%CE%B1%CF%84%CE%B9%CF%8E%CF%84%CE%B7%CF%82_%CE%BC%CE%B5_%CF%84%CE%B7_%CE%BC%CE%B7%CF%84%CE%AD%CF%81%CE%B1_%CF%84%CE%BF%CF%85_%CF%80%CF%81%CE%B9%CE%BD_%CF%84%CE%B7%CE%BD_%CE%B1%CE%BD%CE%B1%CF%87%CF%8E%CF%81%CE%B7%CF%83%CE%B7_%CE%B3%CE%B9%CE%B1_%CF%84%CE%BF_%CE%B1%CE%BB%CE%B2%CE%B1%CE%BD%CE%B9%CE%BA%CF%8C_%CE%BC%CE%AD%CF%84%CF%89%CF%80%CE%BF.jpg)

A Greek woman sees her son depart for the Albanian front.

On 28 October, the Greek army had 14 infantry divisions, one cavalry division and three infantry brigades, all at least partly mobilized since August; four infantry divisions and two brigades were on the border with Albania; five infantry divisions faced Bulgaria and five more with the cavalry division were in general reserve.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoliopoulos1978416-116) Greek army divisions were triangular and held up to 50 per cent more infantry than the Italian binary divisions, with slightly more medium artillery and machine-guns but no tanks.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBuell200237-117) Most Greek equipment was still of First World War issue, from countries like Belgium, Austria, Poland and France, all of which were under Axis occupation, cutting off the supply of spare parts and ammunition. Many senior Greek officers were veterans of a decade of almost continuous warfare, including the [Balkan Wars](/wiki/Balkan_Wars "Balkan Wars") of 1912–13, the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War"), and the [Greco-Turkish War of 1919–22](/wiki/Greco-Turkish_War_of_1919%E2%80%9322 "Greco-Turkish War of 1919–22").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Greece had conscription with all able-bodied males upon turning 18 years old were required to serve for 24 months in the military.[[112]](#cite_note-118)

In Epirus, the 8th Infantry Division was already mobilized and reinforced with a regiment and the staff of the [3rd Infantry Brigade](/w/index.php?title=3rd_Infantry_Brigade_(Greece)&action=edit&redlink=1 "3rd Infantry Brigade (Greece) (page does not exist)"), fielding 15 infantry battalions and 16 artillery batteries. At the time of the Italian attack, the [2/39 Evzone Regiment](/wiki/2/39_Evzone_Regiment "2/39 Evzone Regiment") was moving north from [Missolonghi](/wiki/Missolonghi "Missolonghi") to reinforce the division.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGedeon20019-110) The western Macedonia sector was held by the [Western Macedonia Army Section](/wiki/Western_Macedonia_Army_Section "Western Macedonia Army Section") (TSDM), based at [Kozani](/wiki/Kozani "Kozani") (Lieutenant-General [Ioannis Pitsikas](/wiki/Ioannis_Pitsikas "Ioannis Pitsikas")), with the [II Army Corps](/wiki/II_Army_Corps_(Greece) "II Army Corps (Greece)") (Lieutenant-General [Dimitrios Papadopoulos](/wiki/Dimitrios_Papadopoulos_(general) "Dimitrios Papadopoulos (general)")) and [III Army Corps](/wiki/III_Army_Corps_(Greece) "III Army Corps (Greece)") (Lieutenant-General [Georgios Tsolakoglou](/wiki/Georgios_Tsolakoglou "Georgios Tsolakoglou")), each of two infantry divisions and an infantry brigade. The total forces available to TSDM on the outbreak of war consisted of 22 infantry battalions and 22 artillery batteries (seven heavy). The Pindus sector was covered by the "Pindus Detachment" (Απόσπασμα Πίνδου) (Colonel [Konstantinos Davakis](/wiki/Konstantinos_Davakis "Konstantinos Davakis")) with two battalions, a cavalry company and 1.5 artillery batteries.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGedeon200110-112)

The [Royal Hellenic Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Hellenic_Air_Force "Royal Hellenic Air Force") (*Ellinikí Vasilikí Aeroporía*, RHAF) had to face the numerically and technologically superior *Regia Aeronautica*. It comprised 45 fighters, 24 light bombers, nine reconnaissance aircraft, about 65 auxiliary aeroplanes and 28 naval cooperation aircraft. It consisted of the 21st, 22nd, 23rd and 24th pursuit squadrons, the 31st, 32nd, 33rd bomber squadrons, the 1st, 2nd, 3rd, 4th military cooperation squadrons, the 2828 Independent Military Cooperation Flight and the 11th, 12th and 13th naval cooperation squadrons. At the outbreak of the war the operational combat fleet of the Royal Greek Air Force counted 24 [PZL P.24](/wiki/PZL_P.24 "PZL P.24") and nine [Bloch MB.151](/wiki/Bloch_MB.150 "Bloch MB.150") fighters, as well as eleven [Bristol Blenheim Mk IV](/wiki/Bristol_Blenheim "Bristol Blenheim"), ten [Fairey Battle B.1](/wiki/Fairey_Battle "Fairey Battle") and eight [Potez 633 B2](/wiki/Potez_630 "Potez 630") bombers.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKaisarou-PantazopoulouBeldekosKarytinos200082–90-119) Serviceable ground attack and naval support aircraft included about nine [Breguet 19](/wiki/Breguet_19 "Breguet 19") two-seater biplane bombers, 15 [Henschel Hs 126](/wiki/Henschel_Hs_126 "Henschel Hs 126") reconnaissance and observation aircraft, 17 [Potez 25](/wiki/Potez_25 "Potez 25")A observation aircraft, nine [Fairey III](/wiki/Fairey_III "Fairey III") amphibious reconnaissance aircraft, 12 [Dornier Do 22](/wiki/Dornier_Do_22 "Dornier Do 22")G torpedo bombers, and 9 [Avro Anson](/wiki/Avro_Anson "Avro Anson") maritime reconnaissance aircraft.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShoresCullMalizia198727-120) The main air bases were located in [Sedes](/wiki/Sedes_Airport "Sedes Airport"), [Larissa](/wiki/Larissa_National_Airport "Larissa National Airport"), [Dekeleia](/wiki/Tatoi_Airport "Tatoi Airport"), [Faleron](/wiki/Phalerum "Phalerum"), [Eleusis](/wiki/Eleusis "Eleusis"), [Nea Anchialos](/wiki/Nea_Anchialos_National_Airport "Nea Anchialos National Airport") and [Maleme](/wiki/Maleme_Airport "Maleme Airport").[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKaisarou-PantazopoulouBeldekosKarytinos200082–90-119)

The [Royal Hellenic Navy](/wiki/Royal_Hellenic_Navy "Royal Hellenic Navy") had the elderly cruiser [*Georgios Averof*](/wiki/Greek_cruiser_Georgios_Averof "Greek cruiser Georgios Averof"), two [modern destroyers](/wiki/G_and_H-class_destroyer#Royal_Hellenic_Navy_ships "G and H-class destroyer"), four slightly older [Italian destroyers](/wiki/Freccia-class_destroyer "Freccia-class destroyer") and four obsolete [*Aetos*-class](/wiki/Aetos-class_destroyer "Aetos-class destroyer") [destroyers](/wiki/Destroyer "Destroyer"). There were six old submarines, fifteen obsolete torpedo boats and about thirty other auxiliary vessels.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoliopoulos1978416-116)

### Britain

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/211_Squadron_RAF_Blenheim_Greece_WWII_IWM_CM_288.jpg/250px-211_Squadron_RAF_Blenheim_Greece_WWII_IWM_CM_288.jpg)](/wiki/File:211_Squadron_RAF_Blenheim_Greece_WWII_IWM_CM_288.jpg)

[Bristol Blenheim](/wiki/Bristol_Blenheim "Bristol Blenheim") bomber of [No. 211 Squadron RAF](/wiki/No._211_Squadron_RAF "No. 211 Squadron RAF") at [Menidi](/wiki/Acharnes "Acharnes"), Greece

On 22 October 1940, six days before the Italian invasion of Greece, despite the Italian invasion of Egypt, the RAF [Air Officer Commanding-in-Chief Middle East](/wiki/RAF_Middle_East_Command "RAF Middle East Command") in Cairo was ordered to prepare squadrons for Greece, based on [Ultra](/wiki/Ultra_(cryptography) "Ultra (cryptography)") decodes and other sources that an Italian invasion of Greece was imminent.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHinsley199462–63-121) The RAF first sent [30 Squadron](/wiki/No._30_Squadron_RAF "No. 30 Squadron RAF"), consisting of one flight of [Blenheim](/wiki/Bristol_Blenheim "Bristol Blenheim") IF [night fighters](/wiki/Night_fighter "Night fighter") and one flight of Blenheim I light bombers, that were based at Athens-Eleusis airfield.[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShoresCullMalizia198730–31-122) Soon afterwards, six [Vickers Wellington](/wiki/Vickers_Wellington "Vickers Wellington") medium bombers were detached from [70 Squadron](/wiki/No._70_Squadron_RAF "No. 70 Squadron RAF") and a flight of Blenheim Is from [84 Squadron](/wiki/No._84_Squadron_RAF "No. 84 Squadron RAF") arrived. All RAF assets were placed under the command of [Air vice-marshal](/wiki/Air_vice-marshal "Air vice-marshal") [John D'Albiac](/wiki/John_D%27Albiac "John D'Albiac").[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShoresCullMalizia198732–33,_30–31-123) The RAF aircraft participated in the Greek counter-offensive that began on 14 November, with No. 84 Squadron operating forward from [Menidi](/wiki/Menidi,_Aetolia-Acarnania "Menidi, Aetolia-Acarnania").[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShoresCullMalizia198734–38,_33-124) A few days later, the [Gloster Gladiator](/wiki/Gloster_Gladiator "Gloster Gladiator") fighters of [80 Squadron](/wiki/No._80_Squadron_RAF "No. 80 Squadron RAF") moved forward to [Trikala](/wiki/Trikala "Trikala"), causing significant losses to the *Regia Aeronautica*.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShoresCullMalizia198739-125) [211 Squadron](/wiki/No._211_Squadron_RAF "No. 211 Squadron RAF") with Blenheim Is, followed before the end of November, joining 84 Squadron at Menidi and 80 Squadron moved to [Yannina](/wiki/Ioannina "Ioannina"), about 65 kilometres (40 mi) from the Albanian border. In the first week of December, 14 Gladiators were transferred from the RAF to the RHAF.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShoresCullMalizia198741,_43,_48-126)

## Campaign

The Greek official history of the Greco-Italian War divides it into three periods:[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGedeon200111-127)[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoliopoulos1978414-128)

* The Italian offensive and its failure from 28 October to 13 November 1940
* The Greek counter-offensive, from 14 November to 6 January 1941, the initial Greek counter-offensive in 14–23 November, with the restoration of the pre-war border in Epirus and the capture of Korçë, followed by the Greek advance into Albania until 6 January 1941
* The gradual stabilization of the front from 6 January 1941 until the onset of the German attack on 6 April; the final Greek advances, until 8 March, followed by the Italian spring offensive and the stalemate until April.

The Greek commander-in-chief, [Alexandros Papagos](/wiki/Alexandros_Papagos "Alexandros Papagos"), in his memoirs regarded the second phase as ending on 28 December 1940; as the historian Ioannis Koliopoulos comments, this seems more appropriate, as December marked a watershed in the course of the war, with the Greek counter-offensive gradually grinding to a halt, the German threat becoming clear, and the beginning of British attempts to guide and shape Greek strategy. According to Koliopoulos, the final three months of the war were militarily of little significance as they did not alter the situation of the two combatants, but were mostly dominated by the diplomatic and political developments leading up to the German invasion.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoliopoulos1978416-116)

### Italian offensive (28 October – 13 November 1940)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Italian_Invasion_1940_in_Pindus_Epirus.svg/250px-Italian_Invasion_1940_in_Pindus_Epirus.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Italian_Invasion_1940_in_Pindus_Epirus.svg)

Italian invasion of Greece

Italian forces invaded Greece in several columns. On the extreme Italian right, the coastal group moved south in the direction of [Konispol](/wiki/Konispol "Konispol") with the final aim of capturing [Igoumenitsa](/wiki/Igoumenitsa "Igoumenitsa") and thence driving onto Preveza. In the central sector, the *Siena* Division moved in two columns onto the area of [Filiates](/wiki/Filiates "Filiates"), while the *Ferrara* Division moved in four columns against the main Greek resistance line at Kalpaki with the aim of capturing Ioannina. On the Pindus sector, the *Julia* Division launched five columns aiming to capture Metsovo and cut off the Greek forces in the Epirus sector from the east.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGedeon200111-127) With the onset of the Italian offensive, Papagos, until then the Chief of the [Hellenic Army General Staff](/wiki/Hellenic_Army_General_Staff "Hellenic Army General Staff"), was appointed commander-in-chief of the newly established General Headquarters. The Army General Staff, which functioned as the main field staff throughout the war, was handed over to Lieutenant-General [Konstantinos Pallis](/wiki/Konstantinos_Pallis "Konstantinos Pallis"), recalled from retirement.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGedeon200111–12-129) With Bulgarian neutrality assured—following the terms of the [Balkan Pact](/wiki/Balkan_Pact "Balkan Pact") of 1935, the Turks threatened to intervene on Greece's side if the Bulgarians attacked Greece—the Greek high command was free to throw the bulk of its army against Italian forces in Albania.[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnox1986233-130) Almost half the forces assigned to the Bulgarian front (13th and 17th Divisions, 16th Infantry Brigade) and the entirety of the general reserve ([I Army Corps](/wiki/I_Army_Corps_(Greece) "I Army Corps (Greece)") with 2nd, 3rd, and 4th Infantry Divisions, as well as the Cretan [5th Infantry Division](/wiki/5th_Infantry_Division_(Greece) "5th Infantry Division (Greece)") and the [Cavalry Division](/wiki/Cavalry_Division_(Greece) "Cavalry Division (Greece)")) were directed to the Albanian front.[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoliopoulos1978417-131)

#### Epirus and coastal sectors

See also: [Battle of Elaia–Kalamas](/wiki/Battle_of_Elaia%E2%80%93Kalamas "Battle of Elaia–Kalamas")

On the Epirus sector, Katsimitros had left five battalions along the border to delay the Italian advance, and installed his main resistance line in a convex front with the Kalpaki pass in the centre, manned by nine battalions. Further two battalions under Major-General [Nikolaos Lioumbas](/w/index.php?title=Nikolaos_Lioumbas&action=edit&redlink=1 "Nikolaos Lioumbas (page does not exist)") took over the coastal sector in [Thesprotia](/wiki/Thesprotia "Thesprotia"). The swamps of the Kalamas river, especially before Kalpaki, formed a major obstacle not only to armoured formations, but even to the movement of infantry. A further battalion and some artillery were detached to the Preveza area in the event of an Italian landing, but as this did not materialize, they were swiftly moved to reinforce the coastal sector.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGedeon200113–14-132) By the night of 29/30 October, the Greek covering units had withdrawn to the Kalpaki line, and by 1 November, Italian units made contact with the Greek line. During these three days, the Italians prepared their assault, bombarding the Greek positions with aircraft and artillery. In the meantime, the developing Italian threat in the Pindus sector forced Papagos to cable Katsimitros that his main mission was to cover the Pindus passes and the flanks of the Greek forces in western Macedonia, and to avoid offering resistance if it left his forces depleted. Katsimitros had already decided to defend his line, however, and disregarded these instructions, but detached some forces to cover its right along the [Aoös River](/wiki/Ao%C3%B6s_River "Aoös River").[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGedeon200114–15-133) On 1 November, the Italians managed to capture [Konitsa](/wiki/Konitsa "Konitsa") and the *[Comando Supremo](/wiki/Comando_Supremo "Comando Supremo")* gave the Albanian front priority over Africa.[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnox200080-134)

[![Construction of fortifications at Kalamas](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/Kalamas1939.JPG/250px-Kalamas1939.JPG)](/wiki/File:Kalamas1939.JPG)

Greek soldiers constructing fortifications at Kalamas

The scheduled Italian amphibious assault on Corfu did not materialize due to bad weather. The Italian navy commander, Admiral [Domenico Cavagnari](/wiki/Domenico_Cavagnari "Domenico Cavagnari"), postponed the landing to 2 November, but by that time Visconti Prasca was urgently demanding reinforcements, and Mussolini ordered that the [47th Infantry Division "Bari"](/wiki/47th_Infantry_Division_%22Bari%22 "47th Infantry Division \"Bari\""), earmarked for the operation, be sent to Albania instead.[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnox1986232-135) Mussolini proposed a landing at Preveza on 3 November to break the emerging impasse, but the proposal met with immediate and categorical refusal by the service chiefs.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnox1986234-136)

The main Italian attack on the Kalpaki front began on 2 November. An Albanian battalion, under the cover of a snowstorm, managed to capture the Grabala heights, but were thrown back by a counterattack on the next day. On the same day, an attack spearheaded by 50–60 tanks against the main Kalpaki sector was also repulsed. The Greek units east of the Kalamas were withdrawn during the night. On 5–7 November, repeated assaults were launched against the Grabala and other heights; on the night of the 7th, Grabala briefly fell once more, but was swiftly recaptured. On 8 November, the Italians began withdrawing and assuming defensive positions until the arrival of reinforcements.[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGedeon200115-137) On the coastal sector, the Italians made better progress. The Greek covering units were forced south of the Kalamas already on the first day, but the bad state of the roads delayed the Italian advance. On the night of 4/5 November, the Italians crossed the river and broke through the defences of the local Greek battalion, forcing Lioumbas to order his forces to withdraw south of the [Acheron River](/wiki/Acheron_River "Acheron River"). Igoumenitsa was captured on 6 November, and on the next day, the Italians reached [Margariti](/wiki/Margariti "Margariti"). This marked their deepest advance, as the Thesprotia Sector began receiving reinforcements from Katsimitros, and as on the other sectors the situation had already turned to the Greeks' favour.[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGedeon200117–18-138)

As evidence of the Italian offensive's failure mounted, on 8 November, Visconti Prasca was relieved of overall command in Albania and relegated to command the Italian forces in the Epirus front, while General [Ubaldo Soddu](/wiki/Ubaldo_Soddu "Ubaldo Soddu"), State Undersecretary of War, assumed his place. Soddu's report from Albania underlined Greek resistance in Epirus and the mounting threat of the Greek concentration in western Macedonia, and recommended taking up defensive positions "while awaiting the reinforcements that would permit us to resume action as soon as possible". Mussolini consented.[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnox1986235-139) With the Italians on the defensive, 8th Division began launching local counterattacks to regain the lost ground. By 13 November, the Greek forces once again stood at the Kalamas river along its entire length. On 12 November, I Army Corps under Lieutenant-General [Panagiotis Demestichas](/wiki/Panagiotis_Demestichas "Panagiotis Demestichas") took over the Epirus sector. 8th Division was subordinated to it, while the coastal sector was placed under the independent Lioumbas Detachment.[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKoliopoulos1978417-131)[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGedeon200118-140)

#### Pindus sector

See also: [Battle of Pindus](/wiki/Battle_of_Pindus "Battle of Pindus")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Lot_11618-9_%2824545900475%29.jpg/250px-Lot_11618-9_%2824545900475%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lot_11618-9_(24545900475).jpg)

Greek soldiers operating a field gun
